sillyQuestions.question: How do you recover a deleted unmerged branch with no local copy?
sillyQuestions.answer:
The Answer: Recover the commit first, then recreate the branch pointer. Most of the time, the data is still there. ✅

When a branch is deleted, Git usually removes only the reference (the branch name), not the commit objects immediately. Recovery depends on where the commits still exist.

Fast recovery flow (best to worst):

  1. Check local reflog (if your clone had ever seen the branch):

    git reflog --all --date=iso | grep "<branch-name-or-commit-hint>"

    If you find the commit SHA:

    git checkout -b <recovered-branch> <sha>

  2. Search dangling commits in the repo object database:

    git fsck --lost-found --no-reflogs

    Inspect candidate commits:

    git show <sha>

    Then restore:

    git checkout -b <recovered-branch> <sha>

  3. Check remote hosting refs/history:

    • If the branch had an open PR, the PR often still points to the head commit.
    • CI logs, deployment metadata, or old release notes may contain the commit SHA.

  4. If GitHub branch was deleted and no one has SHA:

    • Check closed/open PRs, workflow logs, and commit search in the repository.
    • As last resort, contact GitHub Support quickly (garbage collection can eventually prune unreachable objects).

Important: Once you recover the SHA, immediately recreate and push:

git checkout -b <recovered-branch> <sha>
git push -u origin <recovered-branch>

Prevention tips:

  • Protect critical branches with deletion restrictions.
  • Require PRs for long-running work.
  • Keep backups/mirrors for high-risk repos.
  • Turn on branch protection + status checks.

In short: branch name gone ≠ work gone. Find the commit SHA, recreate the branch, push it, and protect important branches going forward.

sillyQuestions.question: Why does my code work in my head but not on the computer?
sillyQuestions.answer:
The Answer: You probably forgot to save the file! 🤦‍♂️

This is probably the most common mistake every developer makes, especially when starting out. You spend 30 minutes debugging why your changes aren't working, only to realize you never saved the file.

How to avoid this:

  1. Enable auto-save in your editor (VS Code: File > Auto Save)
  2. Use Ctrl+S (or Cmd+S) religiously after every change
  3. Check the file tab - unsaved files usually have a dot or asterisk
  4. Use hot reload in development - it will force you to save to see changes

Pro tip: Make saving a muscle memory. I save after every few lines of code, even if I'm not done with the thought. It's better to save too often than not enough!

Remember: The computer only knows what you've saved, not what you've typed! 💾

sillyQuestions.question: Why is my JavaScript breaking with 'Unexpected token' errors?
sillyQuestions.answer:
The Answer: You're probably missing a semicolon somewhere! 😅

JavaScript has something called "Automatic Semicolon Insertion" (ASI), but it doesn't always work the way you expect. Sometimes a missing semicolon can cause the next line to be interpreted as part of the previous statement.

Common scenarios:

// This breaks:
let a = 5
let b = 10
[1, 2, 3].forEach(console.log) // Error!

// JavaScript sees this as:
let a = 5let b = 10[1, 2, 3].forEach(console.log)

How to avoid this:

  1. Use a linter like ESLint with semicolon rules
  2. Be consistent - either always use semicolons or never use them
  3. Use Prettier to automatically format your code
  4. Learn the ASI rules if you want to go semicolon-free

My recommendation: Just use semicolons. It's clearer, more explicit, and prevents these weird edge cases. Your future self will thank you! 🙏

sillyQuestions.question: Why isn't my CSS working? I've been staring at it for hours!
sillyQuestions.answer:
The Answer: You probably have a typo in your CSS selector or property name! 🤦‍♂️

This happens to everyone. You write what you think is perfect CSS, but there's a tiny typo that breaks everything.

Common culprits:

  1. Typos in class names:

    /* You wrote */
.btn-primray { color: blue; }

/* But your HTML has */
<button class="btn-primary">Click me</button>

  2. Missing semicolons:

    .my-class {
  color: red  /* Missing semicolon! */
  background: blue;
}

  3. Wrong property names:

    .my-class {
  text-colour: red; /* Should be 'color' */
  font-wieght: bold; /* Should be 'weight' */
}

  4. Selector specificity issues:

    /* This is more specific and overrides your styles */
div.container .my-class { color: green; }

/* Your style gets overridden */
.my-class { color: red; }

How to debug:

  1. Use browser dev tools - Right-click → Inspect Element
  2. Check the computed styles - See what's actually being applied
  3. Look for crossed-out styles - These are being overridden
  4. Validate your CSS - Use a CSS validator to catch syntax errors

Pro tip: Use a good code editor with CSS IntelliSense. It'll catch most typos before you even save the file!

Remember: CSS is case-sensitive for class names but not for property names. background-Color works, but .MyClass and .myclass are different! 🎨

technicalTerms.title: ACID
technicalTerms.description: ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) defines four properties that guarantee database transactions are processed reliably, even during crashes, errors, or concurrent access.

technicalTerms.title: B-Tree
technicalTerms.description: A B-tree is a self-balancing tree data structure that maintains sorted data and allows searches, insertions, and deletions in logarithmic time, forming the foundation of most database indexes.

technicalTerms.title: Backpressure
technicalTerms.description: Backpressure is a flow control mechanism where a slow consumer signals upstream producers to reduce their sending rate, preventing unbounded queue growth and resource exhaustion.

technicalTerms.title: BASE
technicalTerms.description: BASE is an acronym contrasting ACID for distributed systems: Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent. It describes a model where availability is…

technicalTerms.title: Bloom Filter
technicalTerms.description: A Bloom filter is a space-efficient probabilistic data structure that tests set membership, answering "definitely not in set" or "possibly in set" with tunable false positive rates but zero false negatives.

technicalTerms.title: Caching
technicalTerms.description: Caching stores frequently accessed data in a faster storage (often memory) to speed up retrieval.

technicalTerms.title: CAP Theorem
technicalTerms.description: The CAP theorem states that a distributed data store can guarantee at most two of three properties — Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance — when a network partition occurs.

technicalTerms.title: CDC (Change Data Capture)
technicalTerms.description: CDC is the process of tracking and capturing row-level changes (inserts, updates, deletes) in a database and streaming those change events to downstream systems in near real-time.

technicalTerms.title: Checkpointing
technicalTerms.description: Checkpointing is the process of flushing all committed changes from the write-ahead log (WAL) to the main database storage, and then clearing the…

technicalTerms.title: Circuit Breaker
technicalTerms.description: A circuit breaker stops sending requests to a failing downstream service after consecutive failures, preventing cascading failures and giving the service time to recover.

technicalTerms.title: Clustering
technicalTerms.description: In databases, clustering can refer to either grouping similar data together or deploying multiple nodes for scalability/HA. A database cluster is a set of…

technicalTerms.title: Columnar vs Row Storage
technicalTerms.description: Columnar storage organizes data by columns (all values of one field together) while row storage groups complete records together, each optimized for different query patterns.

technicalTerms.title: Compaction
technicalTerms.description: Compaction is the process of merging and recycling data files to reclaim space and improve read performance, especially in Log-Structured Merge (LSM) tree…

technicalTerms.title: Composite Index
technicalTerms.description: A composite index (multi-column index) indexes multiple columns together in a single B-tree, optimizing queries that filter or sort on the combined column set while following the leftmost prefix rule.

technicalTerms.title: Connection Pooling
technicalTerms.description: Connection pooling maintains a pool of open database connections that can be reused, reducing the overhead of opening/closing connections.

technicalTerms.title: Consensus
technicalTerms.description: Consensus is the process by which distributed nodes agree on a single value or decision, even when some nodes fail, using algorithms like Raft and Paxos.

technicalTerms.title: Consistent Hashing
technicalTerms.description: Consistent hashing is a technique to distribute keys across nodes so that minimal keys need to be moved when nodes join/leave.

technicalTerms.title: Covering Index
technicalTerms.description: A covering index is a (secondary or composite) index that includes all columns needed for a query. The index “covers” the query, so the database can…

technicalTerms.title: Data Lake
technicalTerms.description: A data lake is a centralized repository that stores large volumes of raw data in its native format, allowing schema-on-read analysis with tools like Spark, Presto, and Athena.

technicalTerms.title: Data Locality
technicalTerms.description: Data locality is the principle of placing computation (queries, processing) close to where the data resides, rather than moving large data sets across the…

technicalTerms.title: Data Migration
technicalTerms.description: Data migration is the process of moving data between storage systems, formats, or schemas, typically during upgrades or platform changes.

technicalTerms.title: Data Skew
technicalTerms.description: Data skew is an uneven distribution of data or workload across partitions or nodes, causing hotspots where some partitions handle disproportionate traffic or storage.

technicalTerms.title: Data Warehouse
technicalTerms.description: A data warehouse is a centralized analytical store that holds curated, structured data optimized for complex queries and reporting, separate from operational systems.

technicalTerms.title: Deadlock
technicalTerms.description: A deadlock occurs when two or more transactions permanently block each other, each holding a lock the other needs, creating a circular dependency that can only be resolved by aborting one transaction.

technicalTerms.title: Denormalization
technicalTerms.description: Denormalization is the process of intentionally adding redundancy to a database schema to improve read/query performance. In a denormalized design, data…

technicalTerms.title: Dirty Read
technicalTerms.description: A dirty read occurs when a transaction reads data written by another transaction that has not yet committed, risking exposure to data that may be rolled back.

technicalTerms.title: Eventual Consistency
technicalTerms.description: Eventual consistency guarantees that if no new updates are made, all replicas of a data item will converge to the same value over time, trading immediate consistency for higher availability and lower latency.

technicalTerms.title: Exactly-Once Semantics
technicalTerms.description: Exactly-once semantics guarantees that a message or operation is processed once and only once, despite retries, crashes, and network failures.

technicalTerms.title: Failover
technicalTerms.description: Failover is the automatic or manual switching of operations from a failed primary system to a standby replica, ensuring service continuity with minimal downtime.

technicalTerms.title: Federation
technicalTerms.description: Federation is an architecture where multiple autonomous databases or services coordinate to answer queries without sharing a single storage layer.

technicalTerms.title: Geo-Replication
technicalTerms.description: Geo-replication copies data across geographically distributed data centers to improve latency, availability, and disaster recovery.

technicalTerms.title: Hash Partitioning
technicalTerms.description: Hash partitioning distributes data across partitions by applying a hash function to a key, producing uniform distribution for point lookups.

technicalTerms.title: High Availability (HA)
technicalTerms.description: High availability means a system is continuously operational with minimal downtime, typically measured as 99.9% to 99.999% uptime through redundancy, automatic failover, and distributed architecture.

technicalTerms.title: Horizontal Scaling
technicalTerms.description: Horizontal scaling (scale-out) adds more machines to a system to handle increased load, distributing work across nodes rather than upgrading a single server.

technicalTerms.title: Hot Partition (Hot Shard)
technicalTerms.description: A hot partition (or hot shard/key) is a data partition that receives disproportionately high traffic, causing resource saturation.

technicalTerms.title: Idempotency
technicalTerms.description: Idempotency means an operation produces the same result whether executed once or multiple times, making retries safe in distributed systems.

technicalTerms.title: Indexing
technicalTerms.description: A database index is a data structure that improves the speed of data retrieval on a table at the cost of additional writes and storage. It works like a book’s…

technicalTerms.title: Leader–Follower Replication
technicalTerms.description: Leader–Follower (also called master-slave) replication is a strategy where one node (the leader) receives all write operations, and one or more follower…

technicalTerms.title: Load Balancing
technicalTerms.description: Load balancing distributes incoming network requests across multiple servers to prevent overload, improve response times, and provide fault tolerance through redundancy.

technicalTerms.title: Lock Escalation
technicalTerms.description: Lock escalation is the process of converting many fine-grained locks (like row or page locks) into a coarser lock (like a table lock) to reduce lock management…

technicalTerms.title: Logical vs Physical Replication
technicalTerms.description: Logical replication streams row-level change events (inserts, updates, deletes) while physical replication copies raw WAL bytes, producing a byte-identical standby.

technicalTerms.title: LSM Tree
technicalTerms.description: A Log-Structured Merge-tree (LSM tree) is a data structure optimized for high write volumes. It buffers writes in memory and periodically merges them to…

technicalTerms.title: Materialized Views
technicalTerms.description: A materialized view is a database object that stores the result of a query as a physical table. Unlike a regular (virtual) view, it contains actual…

technicalTerms.title: Multi-Leader Replication
technicalTerms.description: Multi-leader (also called multi-master) replication allows multiple nodes (leaders) to accept writes. Data is replicated between leaders to keep them in…

technicalTerms.title: MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control)
technicalTerms.description: MVCC allows multiple transactions to read and write data concurrently without blocking each other, by maintaining multiple versions of each row and giving each transaction a consistent snapshot.

technicalTerms.title: Normalization
technicalTerms.description: Normalization is the process of organizing database schema to reduce redundancy and improve integrity. Typically, data is divided into multiple tables…

technicalTerms.title: Optimistic Locking
technicalTerms.description: Optimistic locking allows concurrent transactions to proceed without acquiring locks, detecting conflicts at commit time using version numbers or timestamps and retrying on collision.

technicalTerms.title: Pessimistic Locking
technicalTerms.description: Pessimistic locking acquires exclusive locks on data before accessing it, preventing concurrent modifications by blocking other transactions until the lock is released.

technicalTerms.title: Phantom Read
technicalTerms.description: A phantom read occurs when a transaction re-executes a query with a range condition and gets a different set of rows because another transaction inserted or deleted matching rows in between.

technicalTerms.title: Query Planner (Cost-Based Optimizer)
technicalTerms.description: The query planner/optimizer is a component that determines the most efficient way to execute a database query. A cost-based optimizer (CBO) estimates the…

technicalTerms.title: Quorum
technicalTerms.description: In distributed systems, a quorum is the minimum number of votes (or nodes) that must agree to perform an operation. It is a technique to ensure consistency…

technicalTerms.title: Range Partitioning
technicalTerms.description: Range partitioning divides data into partitions based on ordered key ranges, keeping contiguous values together for efficient range scans.

technicalTerms.title: Read Replicas
technicalTerms.description: A read replica is a read-only copy of a database instance. The primary database handles writes; read replicas asynchronously replicate data from the primary…

technicalTerms.title: Read Skew
technicalTerms.description: Read skew is a concurrency anomaly where a transaction sees inconsistent data because it reads different rows or tables at different points in time during concurrent modifications.

technicalTerms.title: Rebalancing (Data Rebalancing)
technicalTerms.description: Rebalancing is redistributing data across nodes or partitions to restore even load after cluster changes or skew.

technicalTerms.title: Resharding
technicalTerms.description: Resharding is changing the number or configuration of shards (data partitions) in a sharded database, moving data between shards accordingly.

technicalTerms.title: Schema Evolution
technicalTerms.description: Schema evolution is the practice of modifying a data schema over time while maintaining compatibility with existing readers and writers.

technicalTerms.title: Schema Registry
technicalTerms.description: A schema registry is a centralized service that stores, versions, and enforces compatibility of data schemas in event streaming and messaging systems.

technicalTerms.title: Secondary Indexes
technicalTerms.description: A secondary index is any index on a table column that is not the primary key. It is stored separately from the table rows.

technicalTerms.title: Snapshot Isolation
technicalTerms.description: Snapshot isolation is a transaction isolation level where each transaction operates on a snapshot of the database as of the start of the transaction.…

technicalTerms.title: Split-Brain
technicalTerms.description: Split-brain occurs when a distributed cluster partitions into two or more segments that each believe they are the primary, leading to conflicting writes and data divergence.

technicalTerms.title: Strong Consistency
technicalTerms.description: Strong consistency guarantees that every read returns the result of the most recent write, so all clients see the same data at the same time regardless of which node they query.

technicalTerms.title: Three-Phase Commit (3PC)
technicalTerms.description: Three-phase commit is an extension of 2PC designed to reduce blocking. It adds a “pre-commit” phase so participants can make a safe decision without indefinite…

technicalTerms.title: Throttling and Rate Limiting
technicalTerms.description: Throttling controls resource usage by limiting the rate of requests. Rate limiting enforces a maximum number of requests in a time window to protect services from overload and abuse.

technicalTerms.title: Time-Series Partitioning
technicalTerms.description: Time-series partitioning divides data into partitions by time intervals, optimizing for append-heavy workloads and time-windowed queries.

technicalTerms.title: Two-Phase Commit (2PC)
technicalTerms.description: Two-phase commit is an atomic commitment protocol for distributed transactions. A coordinator asks all involved nodes (participants) whether they can commit.…

technicalTerms.title: Vertical Scaling
technicalTerms.description: Vertical scaling (scale-up) increases the capacity of a single machine by adding CPU, RAM, or faster storage, trading simplicity for a hardware ceiling.

technicalTerms.title: Write Skew
technicalTerms.description: Write skew is a concurrency anomaly where two transactions read overlapping data, then write to non-overlapping rows, producing a state that violates an application invariant neither transaction broke individually.

technicalTerms.title: Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)
technicalTerms.description: Write-ahead logging (WAL) records all changes to a log file before applying them to the database, guaranteeing atomicity and durability by enabling crash recovery through log replay.

tilEntries.title: Docker layer cache order matters — put COPY last
tilEntries.description: Docker caches each instruction as a layer. Once a layer changes, all subsequent layers are rebuilt. Order your Dockerfile so things that change least often come first. **Bad — copies source code before installing dependencies:** ```dockerfile FROM golang:1.23 WORKDIR /app COPY . . # Changes every co

tilEntries.title: Go defer inside a loop doesn't run until the function returns
tilEntries.description: A classic Go gotcha: `defer` in a loop defers until the **surrounding function** returns, not until the loop iteration ends. ```go // BAD: files are not closed after each iteration for _, filename := range files { f, _ := os.Open(filename) defer f.Close() // Only runs when the whole function exits!

tilEntries.title: Kubernetes: liveness vs readiness probes are not the same thing
tilEntries.description: Confusing these two causes subtle production incidents. **Liveness probe** — answers: "Is this container dead and should be restarted?" - Failure → Kubernetes kills and restarts the container - Use for: detecting deadlocks, infinite loops, completely broken state **Readiness probe** — answers: "Is t

tilEntries.title: AWS S3 now provides strong read-after-write consistency
tilEntries.description: Before December 2020, S3 had eventual consistency for overwrite PUTs and DELETEs — meaning you could read stale data immediately after a write. This caught many developers off guard. Since December 2020, **S3 provides strong read-after-write consistency for all operations** — PUTs, DELETEs, and LIST

tilEntries.title: PostgreSQL: JSONB is almost always better than JSON
tilEntries.description: PostgreSQL has two JSON types: `json` and `jsonb`. Always use `jsonb` unless you have a specific reason not to. | Feature | `json` | `jsonb` | |---|---|---| | Storage | Exact text copy | Binary, parsed | | Indexing | Not indexable | GIN/GiST indexable | | Operators | Limited | Full (`@>`, `?`, `#>`)
